comments:
Summary
This paper presents what appears to be the first automatic Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) system for Vietnamese. The authors describe (i) a cleaned subset of the Vietnamese PropBank (≈4.8k single‑predicate sentences), (ii) a new constituent extraction algorithm better suited to Vietnamese bracketed trees (vs. the common 1-1 node mapping), and (iii) a classification pipeline using Maximum Entropy and linear SVMs with novel features (function tags, predicate/head word clusters derived from word embeddings via GMM). The final C-by-C, independent labeling system achieves 73.53% F1 on the cleaned corpus. The authors report feature ablations, comparisons to a 1-1 node mapping baseline, and learning curves. The software and corpus are stated to be released as open source.

Strengths
- Addresses an important gap: Vietnamese SRL had no prior published system; the work provides a useful baseline and resources for the community.
- Presents a clear practical motivation: off-the-shelf methods for English do not port directly to Vietnamese; supported by experiments showing very poor performance of the 1-1 node mapping baseline.
- Proposes a simple, well-explained constituent extraction algorithm tailored to Vietnamese trees, with large empirical gains over the baseline.
- Good experimental coverage: comparison of extraction algorithms, classifiers (ME vs SVM), labelling strategies, feature engineering and ablation, learning curve.
- Promised release of code and corrected corpus is valuable for reproducibility and further research.

Weaknesses / Concerns
- Dataset limitations: experiments are restricted to single‑predicate, relatively short/simple sentences (≈4.8k). Multi‑predicate and complex sentences were discarded or preprocessed out due to annotation errors; it is unclear how the system would generalize to full PropBank-style data.
- Limited baselines: the main extraction baseline is the 1-1 node mapping; the work would be stronger with comparisons to additional alternatives (e.g., word-by-word sequence tagging, dependency-based approaches, or simple neural baselines). No comparison to recent neural SRL methods (even simple baselines) is provided (understandable given 2017 timing but still limits context).
- Lack of detailed error analysis: per-role performance, confusion patterns, and qualitative errors are not presented; this makes it difficult to understand remaining failure modes and where the extraction algorithm or features still fall short.
- Some methodological details are under-specified: how word embeddings were trained (corpus size, epochs, window size), how GMM clustering was configured (number of clusters, initialization), hyperparameters for classifiers (regularization choices beyond defaults), and the process used to fix annotation errors (manual vs automatic) are only briefly sketched.
- Independent labeling model can allow inconsistencies (authors note joint models as future work). No statistical significance testing of reported gains is provided.

Suggestions for improvement
- Provide per-label precision/recall/F1 and confusion matrices to identify common error types and guide future work.
- Include more details about embedding and clustering training (data, hyperparameters), and about corpus cleaning/editing procedures. If manual fixes were made, document them carefully.
- Evaluate on multi-predicate/complex sentences (even if performance is lower) or provide an analysis/explanation of how to extend to that setting.
- Add comparisons to additional baselines (e.g., sequence tagging, simple neural BiLSTM tagger) and/or a joint model or post-hoc constrained decoding (ILP) to reduce labeling inconsistencies.
- Report statistical significance for the main reported improvements.

Verdict
This is a solid and useful engineering/empirical contribution: it provides the first Vietnamese SRL system, a tailored extraction algorithm, and a detailed empirical study with a publicly available corpus and code. It is appropriate for arXiv and will be of interest to practitioners and researchers working on Vietnamese or under-resourced languages. With additional details and expanded experiments (some of which are noted above), the paper would be even stronger. I recommend acceptance and dissemination; the work represents good baseline material for the community.
